metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the new GitHub Release job must carry the same `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` guard as the existing `publish` job, so `workflow_dispatch` dry-runs continue to work without attempting to create a release.
findings.finding: The plan's AC2 requires the new GitHub Release job to fire 'on a v* tag push' but does not specify an `if:` guard to suppress it on `workflow_dispatch` dry-run executions. The existing `publish` job already uses `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` to prevent spurious dry-run publishes, but no equivalent guard is described for the new release job.
findings.location: AC line 2 / Fix section 'Process (code, via Gerrit)' / .github/workflows/release.yml publish job (line 53)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the new job definition: `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` — mirroring the guard already on the `publish` job.
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify an idempotency strategy for `gh release create` (e.g., `--clobber` flag or a pre-check `gh release view` gate) so the workflow can be safely re-run without manual cleanup.
findings.finding: The plan does not address idempotency of `gh release create`: if the release workflow job is re-run after a partial failure (or triggered twice for the same tag), the command will error with 'release already exists', breaking the workflow. This is a MAJOR missing idempotency guarantee on a write operation.
findings.location: AC: `.github/workflows/release.yml` creates a GitHub Release automatically on a `v*` tag push
findings.suggested_fix: Add `--clobber` to the `gh release create` invocation, or prefix the step with `gh release view $TAG 2>/dev/null || gh release create $TAG ...` to skip creation if it already exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief RETIRE/UPDATE procedure to `docs/releasing.md` covering: how to delete or mark a GitHub Release as a pre-release/draft after the fact, and who owns that action.
findings.finding: The plan has no RETIRE/cleanup path for GitHub Releases: if a release is created for a bad tag (e.g., a botched patch), there is no documented procedure for deleting or superseding it, and ownership of that action is undefined.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion (or a named verification step) that exercises the new release.yml job end-to-end: e.g., after the workflow merges, push a lightweight test tag (`v0.6.1-rc.0` or a dedicated test tag) and confirm GitHub automatically creates a Release, then delete it — or document an explicit rationale for why a post-merge smoke-push is not needed.
findings.finding: The new workflow job (AC2) has no acceptance criterion or described step that EXERCISES the new code path end-to-end in production — i.e., no step calls for pushing a real (or test) v* tag after the workflow lands to confirm GitHub automatically creates a release. The backfill ops step proves old releases exist but does not trigger the new workflow, so a broken `release.yml` addition could go undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'Verified by pushing tag `v0.6.1-test` after the workflow merges; a GitHub Release is automatically created and then deleted, confirming the job fires correctly.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact set of existing `v*` git tags (e.g., `git tag -l 'v*' | sort -V`) and reconcile against the AC's backfill list (v0.4.0, v0.5.0, v0.5.1, v0.5.2, v0.6.0) before the ops step is executed.
findings.finding: The backfill AC lists specific tags (v0.4.0, v0.5.0, v0.5.1, v0.5.2, v0.6.0) as assertions about live git tag state, but no tracked file in the repo references v0.5.1 or v0.5.2 — these patch-version tags cannot be verified from the codebase. If the actual tag inventory differs (e.g., a tag is missing, misspelled, or additional patch versions exist), the backfill AC is wrong and the ops step will either miss a release or attempt a non-existent tag.
findings.location: AC line 1: 'GitHub Releases exist for v0.4.0, v0.5.0, v0.5.1, v0.5.2, v0.6.0'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a pre-step in the ops runbook: `git fetch --tags && git tag -l 'v*' | sort -V` to confirm the canonical tag list, then update the AC's explicit version enumeration to match what `git tag` actually reports.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before executing the backfill, verify current GitHub Release state with `gh release list` and confirm v0.3.0 is still Latest and which releases are absent, then adjust the ops step accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'github.com/navapbc/rebar shows v0.3.0 as Latest' and 'v0.2.0/v0.3.0 GitHub Releases were created by hand' as current live GitHub state, but these are external API/UI facts unverifiable from the codebase. No file references v0.2.0 or v0.3.0. If someone has already manually corrected the Latest pointer or created interim releases since the plan was written, the problem statement's premise (and thus the urgency of the backfill) may be stale.
findings.location: Plan 'What' section: 'github.com/navapbc/rebar shows v0.3.0 as Latest while tags and all three channels ... advanced to v0.6.0'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `gh release list --limit 20` as the very first step in the backfill runbook, with a note to skip any tags for which a release already exists (or use `gh release create --notes-from-tag` with an idempotency guard).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the failure mode where PyPI publishing succeeds but GitHub Release creation fails, and specify whether the release job runs after (depends-on) the PyPI job and how operators are notified of failure (e.g., workflow failure email, Slack alert).
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what happens when the GitHub Release creation step fails after PyPI publishing has already succeeded: the two operations are not atomic, leaving the system in a partially-released state (package on PyPI, no GitHub Release) with no documented recovery path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the new GitHub Release job must declare `needs: publish` (not just `needs: build`) so a GitHub Release is only created after PyPI publish succeeds, preventing a release being advertised before its artifacts are available.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify that the new GitHub Release job should depend on (`needs:`) the `publish` job succeeding. If PyPI publish fails after a tag is pushed, the GitHub Release would still be created, publicly advertising a version that does not exist on PyPI — the opposite of the problem being solved.
findings.location: Fix section / AC: `.github/workflows/release.yml` creates a GitHub Release automatically on a `v*` tag push
findings.suggested_fix: Add `needs: publish` to the new GitHub Release job, and mirror the same `if: github.event_name == 'push' && startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v')` guard so it also doesn't run on `workflow_dispatch` dry-runs.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
